blurb: An upright clump of cream-margined variegated leaves in whorls, hung in early summer with whorls of starry yellow flowers — not to be confused with the noxious purple loosestrife (a different genus), though it too runs by rhizome and wants containing.
sun: Full sun to part shade (laxer and less aggressive in dry shade)
water: Moderate; likes rich, moist soil and tolerates wet ground
spread: Runs by rhizome — can be aggressive; contain it
seasons: Cream-and-green variegated whorled foliage spring–fall (pink-flushed when new); whorls of yellow star flowers early-to-late summer
wildlife: Yellow flowers draw bees — including oil-collecting Macropis bees — and butterflies
deer: Not well documented as deer-resistant; grown for foliage and flowers
origin: SE Europe & W Asia (Balkans to Turkey/Caucasus)

care.hardiness: Cold-hardy through USDA zones 4–8, so fully winter-hardy in Boulder's 5b–6a; it dies back to the ground each fall and returns from the rhizome in spring.[1][2]
care.planting: Plant in spring after the mid-May frost or in early fall in moisture-retentive soil, where it establishes easily. Give it room, or a spot where you can control its spread, and keep container plants well watered.[1][2]
care.sun: Full sun to part shade. It flowers best and stays sturdiest in sun with steady moisture; in dry shade it grows laxer and spreads more slowly.[1][2]
care.soil: Wants rich, moisture-retentive soil but tolerates clay and a wide pH range from acid to alkaline; it even shrugs off wet ground.[1][2]
care.water: Moderate and steady — this is a damp-meadow plant, so it wants more water than a xeric bed provides; the drier it is the less it runs, but too dry and it scorches and flowers poorly.[1][2]
care.spacing: Space 12–18 in apart; the rhizomes fill in and can push outward, so leave room or plan to divide.[1]
care.propagation: Division in spring or fall is easiest — the running rhizomes yield abundant divisions. The variegated 'Alexander' only occasionally sets seed and will not come true from it, so propagate by division.[1][2]
care.bloom: Cup-shaped yellow flowers with an orange-red eye ring the leaf whorls from roughly June into late summer, held tight against the upright stems.[1][2]
care.selfsow: The variegated form rarely seeds; its spread is almost entirely by creeping rhizome rather than by seedlings.[2]
care.maintenance: Cut the stems to the ground in late fall or early spring. The ongoing job is containment: the rhizomes can colonize, so divide or root-prune the clump every couple of years, plant it where it can be edged, and keep it away from wet natural areas where loosestrifes can naturalize.[1][2]
care.troubles: Its only real fault is that vigorous rhizomatous spread; also watch for the occasional reverted all-green shoot on the variegated form and cut it out so it doesn't overtake the variegated growth.[1][2]

references.name: NC State Extension Plant Toolbox — Lysimachia punctata

edible.summary: Not a food plant — dotted loosestrife is an ornamental with no established edible use.[1]
edible.caution: Grown for its variegated foliage and flowers, not for eating; no part is recommended as food. Note this is Lysimachia punctata, NOT the invasive purple loosestrife (Lythrum salicaria, a Colorado List A noxious weed) — an entirely different genus.[1][2]

gaps: Some photos are species-representative green-leaved Lysimachia punctata; the cream-variegated 'Alexander' foliage is shown where a CC image exists.
